coredata.dc:title: Converting waste Allium sativum peel to nitrogen and sulphur co-doped photoluminescence carbon dots for solar conversion, cell labeling, and photobleaching diligences: A path from discarded waste to value-added products
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Poushali
Ganguly, Sayan
Maity, Priti Prasanna
Srivastava, Hemant Kumar
Bose, Madhuparna
Dhara, Santanu
Bandyopadhyay, Sharba
Das, Amit Kumar
Banerjee, Susanta
Das, Narayan Chandra
coredata.dc:description: Proper waste utilization in order to promote value added product is a promising scientific practice in recent era. Inspiring from the recurring trend, we propose a single step oxidative pyrolysis derived fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from Allium sativum peel, which is a natural, nontoxic, and waste raw material. Because of its excellent optical properties, and photostability this C-dots have been used in versatile area of applications. Due to its immediate water dispersing character, C-dots reinforced Poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) films revealed improvement in uniaxial stretching behavior and can be used as transparent sunlight conversion film. The nanocomposite film has been tested against rigorous simulated sunlight which proved almost identical sunlight conversion behavior with no photo-bleachable character which is definitely added an extra quality of transparent polymer films. Moreover, the C-dots dispersion has been used as in vitro biomarker for living cells owing to its ease in solubility, biocompatibility, non-cytotoxicity and bright fluorescence even in subcutaneous environment. For this case, adipose derived mesenchymal stem cells (ADMSCs) have been chosen and injected to rabbit ear skin to perform two-photon imaging experiment. The present work opens a new avenue towards the large-scale synthesis of bio-waste based fluorescent C-dots, paving the way for their versatile applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Poly (acrylic acid)
Photobleaching
Solar conversion effect
Two-photon imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots from Waste Allium sativum Peels (GC-Dots)
Preparation of GC-dots/PAA Film, and Glass Vial Coating
Morphology and Structural Properties of the C-dots
Cytotoxicity Study and Cell-Labeling Assay of AD-MSCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GC-dots assembled on a Cu grid coated with an ultrathin amorphous carbon film. (b) Particle size distribution histogram of the GC-dots. Inset showed the SAED pattern.
(a) FTIR spectrum of the GC-dots (b) XPS spectra of wide scan survey of GC-dots. High resolution scans of (c) C1s region and (d) N1 s region for GC-dots nanostructures, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the GC-dots in aqueous dispersion, inset: photograph taken under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (b) Emission spectra of the GC-dots at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 440 nm, inset: normalized emission spectra. (d) Effect on FL intensity of the GC-dots (0.01 g/mL) at different pH values (λex = 380 nm). (e) FL decay profile of the as-prepared GC-dots.
Schematic approach used to prepare GC-dots and its multifunctional appliacations.
(a) Typical stress-strain plot and (b) elongation at break of pure PAA and other GC-dots loaded PAA nanocomposites. (c) Schematic illustrations of the tensile strength enhancement behaviour.

(a) FT-IR spectra of PAA film and GC-dots/PAA film. Absorption spectra of (b) pure PAA film (c) GC-dots/PAA film, respectively. Inset of (b) and (c) showed the pure PAA film and GC-dots incorporated PAA film, respectively. (d) Schematic diagram for the preparation method of the GC-dots/PAA composite and its application in sunlight conversion effect and photo-bleaching applications.
GC-dots/PAA composite films under UV light. Fluorescent composite films were in state of (a) bending (b) half rolling (c) rolling, and (d) strip, respectively.

(a) The sunlight conversion effect of the GC-dots/PAA film. (b) Transmittance spectra of the GC-dots/PAA composite film with different concentration of GC-dots. (c) Bleaching experiment using Rhodamine B in a GC-dots/PAA solution coated (left), uncoated (middle), and PAA coated (right) glass vial.

(a) Cytotoxicity of the GC-dots. Fluorescence microscopic images of adipose derived stem cells labeled with GC-dots using excitation filters (b) 330–380 nm (c) 450–490 nm (d) 510–560 nm using ZEN software. Scale bar 25 μm.
(a) Two photon imaging of intravenously inoculation GC-dots in in vivo and (b) subcutaneously inoculated GC-dots labeled stem cell. Excitation wavelength was at 800 nm.

Fluorescence microscopy images of ADMSCs after incubation with GC-dots at (a-c) 37οC, (d-f) 4οC; ADMSCs pretreated with (j-l) Filipin III, and (g-i) chlorpromazine, then incubated with GC-dots. Scale bar 25 μm.
Schematic diagram of cell imaging using GC-dots nano-probe.
Synthesis process of the GC-dots and its multifunctional applications in photobleaching, solar conversion, and in vitro cell imaging
Comparison of different types of C-dots synthesis.

head.ce:title: Converting waste Allium sativum peel to nitrogen and sulphur co-doped photoluminescence carbon dots for solar conversion, cell labeling, and photobleaching diligences: A path from discarded waste to value-added products

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Proper waste utilization in order to promote value added product is a promising scientific practice in recent era. Inspiring from the recurring trend, we propose a single step oxidative pyrolysis derived fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from Allium sativum peel, which is a natural, nontoxic, and waste raw material. Because of its excellent optical properties, and photostability this C-dots have been used in versatile area of applications. Due to its immediate water dispersing character, C-dots reinforced Poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) films revealed improvement in uniaxial stretching behavior and can be used as transparent sunlight conversion film. The nanocomposite film has been tested against rigorous simulated sunlight which proved almost identical sunlight conversion behavior with no photo-bleachable character which is definitely added an extra quality of transparent polymer films. Moreover, the C-dots dispersion has been used as in vitro biomarker for living cells owing to its ease in solubility, biocompatibility, non-cytotoxicity and bright fluorescence even in subcutaneous environment. For this case, adipose derived mesenchymal stem cells (ADMSCs) have been chosen and injected to rabbit ear skin to perform two-photon imaging experiment. The present work opens a new avenue towards the large-scale synthesis of bio-waste based fluorescent C-dots, paving the way for their versatile applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
Poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) is a high-molecular weight polymer of acrylic acid. Because of its non-toxicity after polymerization, biocompatibility, and hydrophilic nature, PAA has been capturing extensive attention to the researchers. Recently, numerous efforts has been made in the synthesis of hybrid composites based on PAA with prospective application [
37
,
38
]. However, till now the sunlight conversion features of the hybrid film using PAA and C-dots have not been reported.

In this work, we developed a green synthetic technique aimed to prepare nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S) co-doped C-dots from the waste skins of Allium sativum for the first time by simple pyrolysis method. This technique avoids toxic or costly precursors and solvents. It provides an effective path to reclaim kitchen waste which does not need yield reducing or time consuming steps. These C-dots showed green fluorescence under UV light with narrow size distribution, good biocompatibility, and excellent photostability. We fabricated PAA and C-dots based transparent sunlight conversion film for the very first time which unveils very high (99%) optical transparency at 700 nm. Moreover, from the economic standpoints, in comparison to commercially available sunlight conversion agent (rare earth irons doped phosphors), the original material used here rather easily available and waste material involving in the synthesis of C-dots. Also, the PAA stabilized C-dots dispersion has been studied for photo-bleaching experiment by coating with glass vials containing Rhodamine B solution. Moreover, the in vitro and in vivo studies suggest that these zero dimensional C-dots may reveal a new pathway for biomedical uses. Overall, these findings open up a new class of C-dots that are biocompatible and potentially can be employed in a wide range of multifunctional applications.

Allium sativum (garlic) were purchased from the Technology Market, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), Poly(acrylic acid), and quinine sulfate (>99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots from Waste Allium sativum Peels (GC-Dots)
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence C-dots were prepared through simple pyrolysis method. Allium sativum peels were collected and washed several times with water. They were dried in sunlight and put into a ceramic crucible and carbonized at the temperature of 315 °C for 3 h. After cooling down to room temperature the black products were mechanically grounded with a mortar and pestle to obtain a fine powder. Then, 0.1 g of the resultant sample was added into 10 mL of deionized (DI) water and ultrasonically (sonics VCX 750, 750 W, 20 kHz) dispersed for 15 min to form a black solution. The C-dots were collected through filtration using a filtration membrane (0.22 μm pore size) by removing larger particles. Further, the obtained solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off: 1000 Da) against water. The kitchen waste derived GC-dots solution was collected and kept at 4 °C for further characterization and use. Scheme 1 illustrated the synthesis procedure of the GC-dots and applications in photobleaching, solar conversion and in vitro cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GC-dots/PAA Film, and Glass Vial Coating
ce:section.ce:para: Sunlight conversion film is composed of two parts: sunlight conversion agent and matrix. Prior to the fabrication of GC-dots/PAA composite sunlight conversion film, C-dots were synthesized by simple pyrolysis of Allium sativum peels. For this system PAA was used as matrix, which is non-toxic and hydrophilic in nature. In brief, 0.21 g of PAA was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water at 60 °C under continuous stirring until a transparent solution was obtained. After that, 800 μL of C-dots (0.07 g/mL) solution was added and the solution was stirred for 60 min at 60 °C. The obtained solution was then centrifuged under ambient condition at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The solution was quickly casted into a petri dish and dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 24 h. The aqueous solution was used as solvent and no toxic gas was produced in the course of film preparation. Hence, this technique is an eco-friendly and efficacious approach to prepare sunlight conversion film. This result is in agreement with the production of commercial scale and expected to have prospective application in agricultural planting. A glass vial was dipped into the C-dots/PAA solution and dried at 50 °C under vacuum.

All animal experiments were approved by Institutional Animal Ethics Committee of the Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur. New Zealand white male rabbit (3.5–4 kg) was used. Initially, the rabbit was anaesthetised with 5% Isoflurane before the experiment. For injecting the labeled cells into the rabbit ear skin, skin hair was removed using Veet® with a cotton swab, and wiped with 70% ethanol. A metal plate was glued to the skin to make it stable during the two photon imaging. The GC-dots labeled cells were injected using a 20 g needle. Imaging was done using a two-photon laser-scanning microscope (Prairie Technologies) with a Spectra Physics Insight DS laser at 800 nm. Depth: 170–200 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and Structural Properties of the C-dots

ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical property of polymer nanocomposites is commonly evaluated by measuring their tensile property. Different concentrations of the GC-dots loaded PAA composite films were studied (Fig.S8). In general PAA lies in the domain of plastic family. As shown in Fig. 5a,b pure PAA showed tensile stress value of around 0.24 MPa and very low elongation at break (EB). After incorporation of GC-dots into PAA by simple solution mixing assisted with ultrasonication the tensile and EB were increased. Surface functionalities of the GC-dots are the major anchoring components which enhances the mechanical property of GC-dots/PAA nanocomposites. As per the functional group characterizations it is seen that the C-dots are highly abundant with polar groups in their surfaces. Intermolecular H-bonding between GC-dots and PAA affects primarily the increment in tensile strength. This trend follows a monotonic increment in tensile strength of the nanocomposite films. But after 0.07 g/mL the trend interrupted by lowering the tensile strength which was prominent at 0.1 g/mL GC-dots loading into PAA matrix. In case of 0.1 g/mL loading, the elastic modulus enhanced but the elongation at break was downgraded. The increment in tensile strength after GC-dots addition was attributed by the synergic effect of H-bonding and physisorption of PAA chains onto GC-dots surfaces. Physisorped macromolecular chains generally adhered to the C-dots surfaces which are termed as ‘tightly bound polymer’ which is schematically designated in Fig. 5c. The polymer chains just above this tightly bound polymer layer can be called as the ‘immobilized polymer’. Besides these layers the polymer matrix is normally the usual un-bound polymer or free polymer. The implication of C-dots in polymer matrix is most prominently seen when the whole polymer film was experienced uniaxial tension. During tension, the PAA chains are almost completely decoiled on the verge of rupture. But after addition of C-dots, the adsorped PAA chains forms distinct islands which can be called ‘fix points’. These fix points actually act as physical crosslinking points as well stress transfer points in the nanocomposites (Fig. 5c). But after a critical concentration (here 0.07 g/mL) the C-dots are become close to each other due to their high surface energy and forms agglomerate. Hence, poor dispersion of C-dots in PAA matrix diminished the number of stress transfer points which results failure of the nanocomposite films due to improper stress distribution throughout the nanocomposite.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of PAA film, GC-dots and GC-dots/PAA film were performed. The pure PAA film (Fig. 6b) showed an absorption peak around 291 nm but the GC-dots/PAA film displayed (Fig. 6c) continuous broad spectrum from 278 to 310 nm (ultraviolet region). The absorption spectrum of the GC-dots/PAA film suggests the good combination of both the GC-dots and PAA in the composites. We can say that the optical properties of the GC-dots have not changed noticeably after embedded in the PAA matrix. Therefore, PAA can perform as a suitable matrix for the experiment of sunlight conversion.

PAA is a kind of hydrophilic polymer, holding several hydroxyl groups. The C-dots are also containing lots of polar groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, nitrogen and sulphur functional groups) at the surfaces. Therefore, PAA can efficiently adsorb C-dots via the polar functional group anchoring with pendant carboxylic acid groups present in PAA. The present synthesis method conveyed a green system to fabricate sunlight conversion films, without producing toxic fluid and vapor. To examine the sunlight conversion effect of the hybrid film better, sunlight was imitated by a 100 W Xe lamp as light source. For comparison study, the Xe light spectrum was measured from 300 to 800 nm. Afterthat, the light passes through the composite film vertically and the transmission spectrum was recorded at the same condition. The result of the sunlight conversion was represented in Fig. 8a. From the Figure it can be seen that the below 400 nm the intensity of ultraviolet light decreases remarkably owing to the strong absorption property of GC-dots whereas the green light (400–500 nm) develops a propensity to ascend compared to the light source. From the above outcomes, we can conclude that GC-dots/PAA film can efficiently absorb ultraviolet light and convert it to green light, giving the ideal sunlight conversion properties. In addition, to ensure plant's absorption of visible light the composite film required to offer excellent optical transmission. The transmittance of the GC-dots/PAA film is needed to be higher than 99% at 700 nm wavelength. In the regions of 232–700 nm wavelengths, the transmittance spectra of the composite film with various concentrations of the GC-dots were recorded. The pure PAA film shows optical transmission (Fig. 8b) about 99.94829% at 700 nm wavelength. Surprisingly, the optical transmission was almost same (99.93691%) after addition of GC-dots in the PAA matrix. The optical transmission value was >99% in the visible light region. To investigate the applicability of aqueous GC-dots/PAA solution in potential applications, a glass vial was dip coated in GC-dots/PAA solution and after that drying in an oven. The GC-dots/PAA coating hardly decreased the visible transparency of the vial. The coated and uncoated vial (for positive control) were exposed to UV light (365 nm) for 3 h filled with solution containing Rhodamine B. For the uncoated vial solution extensive photobleaching was noticed whereas the coated vial solution still retained its purple color (Fig. 8c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity Study and Cell-Labeling Assay of AD-MSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to application in cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of the GC-dots was measured using MTT assay method. Results demonstrated in Fig. 9a shows that the C-dots unveiled low cytotoxicity with >92% cells retaining viability when incubated in the medium containing GC-dots solution. This study suggested that the GC-dots were non-toxic and shows good biocompatibility with stem cells. From the detailed characterizations, the GC-dots unveiled good photostability in different conditions such as wide range of pH, high ionic strength, different physiological conditions and also its retains its fluorescence activity even after long term storage. These benefits of small sized water dispersible GC-dots along with good biocompatibility and nontoxic behavior suggested that these nanodots can be suitable for biological applications. The GC-dots treated stem cells at 37οC were visualized under fluorescence microscope to explore the biolabeling application. Fig. 9(b-d) demonstrated that the photoluminescence of the GC-dots penetrates into the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area of the cells. The C-dots labeled stem cells showed bright fluorescence at the corresponding excitation wavelength. It clearly illustrated the advantage of excitation dependent features of the GC-dots for bioimaging by means of providing different alternative excitation or emission wavelengths. The brightfield image of stem cell without the treatment of GC-dots showed in Fig.S10 whereas in different excitation filter the cell does not exhibited any fluorescence signal.

To examine the photo-stability for cell imaging overtime we performed the experiment after different time interval. The GC-dots incubated cells were imaged under fluorescence microscopy at 0 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min. Staining ADMSCs with GC-dots led to strong PL emission from the cells. The GC-dots labeled cells yielded stable fluorescent signals during 90 min continuous observation (Fig.S11). Under microscope no significant damage was detected in GC-dots treated cells without any photo bleaching for a long time period. The GC-dots were mostly incorporated into the cytoplasm region surrounding the nucleus. This result showed that GC-dots unveiled good stability in intracellular system over a long time. Even with a number of runs of repeated excitation, FL intensity in the microscopic images remains unchanged which indicates high photostability of the GC-dots in the cells. The successful labeling of stem cells certifies promising prospective of the C-dots obtained from kitchen waste as a broad-spectrum fluorescent nano-probe for in vitro and in vivo imaging. Further, to test the efficiency of the GC-dots, we performed the experiment evaluating signal intensity in vivo. The in vivo ADMSCs tacking capability of the GC-dots was studied on a New Zealand white male rabbit model by employing two photon fluorescence microscope.

In summary, we have illustrated a green synthetic route to prepare luminescent S and Nk doped C-dots by pyrolysis using waste material (garlic husk) as the precursor. The aqueous solution of the C-dots emits strong green light under UV irradiation, and the emission wavelength was red shifted under longer excitation wavelength. Afterthat, a simple technique was developed towards sunlight conversion film via effective dispersion of GC-dots in PAA solution. The composite film unveiled excellent mechanical and optical properties which can efficiently convert ultraviolet light into green light due to their characteristic optical properties. Also, the GC-dots/PAA composite revealed excellent photobleaching resistance towards Rhodamine B. This article not only provides a newfangled natural source to synthesize dual doped C-dots, but also showed multifunctional applications of C-dots in sunlight conversion, photobleaching effect, and in vitro imaging of AD-MSCs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2019.111545.
